title: PC Replacement Survey: One Size Fits None
author: Peter S. Kastner (Aberdeen Group / InternetWeek)
abstract: First-person Kastner column published December 23, 2003 in InformationWeek (originally authored for InternetWeek) reporting results of a joint Aberdeen Group / InternetWeek reader survey on business-PC replacement practices. 250+ respondents manage ~750,000 business PCs, with the top-10 alone covering 550,000 users. Kastner reports six deals of 10,000+ PCs totaling 70,000 in the last twelve months and three companies now buying 30,000 PCs each; 43% of desktops and 54% of laptops are replaced ad hoc; the 36-month replacement schedule is converging with 42-month laptops. Byline credits Kastner as 'the Aberdeen Group' and his full title is EVP and Chief Research Officer for the Consumer Digital Technology Practice at Aberdeen.
importance_rationale: Primary first-person analyst memoir documenting the definitive 2003 turn of the corporate PC buying cycle, with sharp data (six 10K+-PC deals, 70K total; three 30K-PC deals forward-looking; 160K total pipeline) and a clear 36-month convergence thesis.
relevance_rationale: Survey-based framing of enterprise PC lifecycle and ad-hoc-vs-scheduled trade-off remains applicable; specific 2003 figures are historical.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's PC buying-drought-fading call was right — corporate PC spending recovered strongly through 2004-2007; 3-year desktop replacement cycle became the industry norm through the 2010s.
